content.text: GenAI Troubleshooting Runbook Maintenance

A troubleshooting runbook is only useful in an incident if it is accurate at the time the incident occurs. A runbook that describes an alert that was renamed, references a dashboard that was restructured, or gives a remediation command that no longer works does not reduce mean time to recovery — it increases it, because the on-call engineer must simultaneously diagnose the incident and figure out what the runbook is trying to say.

GenAI services generate a category of runbooks that are particularly prone to staleness: runbooks that describe model behavior (output quality degradation, safety filter behavior, prompt injection patterns) change whenever the model is updated. These runbooks require maintenance practices beyond the standard 'verify once per quarter' approach.

attrs.alt: GenAI troubleshooting runbook standard structure (left) and maintenance lifecycle (right) from incident firing through quarterly review, change-triggered updates, and fire drills

content.text: What Makes a Runbook Maintainable

A runbook that is hard to maintain is one that describes symptoms in terms of internal implementation details that change frequently, embeds absolute values (memory thresholds, timeout durations) that are tuned over time, or references people by name rather than role. Before establishing a maintenance cadence, the runbooks themselves must be structured for maintainability.
Maintainable Runbook Principles
Symptom-first, not implementation-first

: describe what the user or monitoring system observes, not what the internal code is doing. 'Inference latency p95 > 2000ms' is stable; 'model_forward_pass() taking > 500ms' becomes stale when the function is renamed.
Parameterize thresholds

: reference threshold names from the alert definition, not hardcoded values. 'latency exceeds the LATENCY_P95_SLO_BREACH_THRESHOLD alert definition' stays accurate when the threshold is tuned; '> 2000ms' requires updating every runbook when the SLO changes.
Role-based escalation, not name-based
: 'escalate to the ML on-call engineer' not 'call Alice Chen'. Names change quarterly; roles are stable.
Embed dashboard and query links
: direct links to the relevant Grafana panel, CloudWatch query, or Kibana saved search — not instructions to 'go look at the dashboard and find the relevant chart.'
Copy-pasteable commands
: every command must work without modification given a correctly configured environment. No commands with placeholder text that the engineer must understand and replace.
Runbook Template

# Runbook: Inference Latency SLO Breach

## Metadata
- **Alert**: `INFERENCE_LATENCY_P95_SLO_BREACH`
- **Severity**: P2
- **SLA**: Acknowledge within 15 minutes; mitigation within 1 hour
- **Last reviewed**: 2025-11-01 by alice.chen@company.com
- **Review cadence**: 90 days + on any alert reconfiguration

## Symptom
Inference latency p95 has exceeded the SLO threshold for 5 consecutive minutes.
The alert fires from Prometheus via AlertManager; it will also page via PagerDuty.

**Dashboard**: [Inference Latency Overview](https://grafana.internal/d/genai-inference/latency)

## Initial Triage (first 5 minutes)

1. Check the dashboard link above. Identify whether:
   - Latency is elevated across ALL regions → likely model or infrastructure issue
   - Latency is elevated in ONE region → likely regional infrastructure issue
   - Latency is elevated for ONE model → likely model-specific issue

2. Check for concurrent incidents in #incidents Slack channel.
   If another incident is already open that explains this, link to it and monitor.

## Diagnostic Steps

**If all regions elevated:**

Step A: Check GPU utilization
```bash
kubectl exec -n inference $(kubectl get pods -n inference -o name | head -1)   -- nvidia-smi --query-gpu=utilization.gpu,memory.used --format=csv,noheader
```
Expected: < 90% GPU utilization, < 75% memory.
If > 90%: go to [High GPU Utilization runbook](./high-gpu-utilization.md)

Step B: Check for pending request queue buildup
```bash
kubectl exec -n inference $(kubectl get pods -n inference -o name | head -1)   -- curl -s http://localhost:9090/metrics | grep inference_queue_depth
```
Expected: < 50 pending requests per pod.
If > 50: go to Step C.
If < 50 but latency still high: escalate to ML on-call (see Escalation below).

Step C: Scale out inference deployment
```bash
# Current replica count:
kubectl get deployment inference-service -n inference -o jsonpath='{.spec.replicas}'

# Scale out by 4 replicas (requires SRE or ML on-call approval for > 8 replicas):
kubectl scale deployment inference-service -n inference --replicas=<CURRENT+4>
```
Wait 3 minutes for pods to become ready. Verify latency is recovering on dashboard.

## Remediation

If scaling out resolves the issue:
- Monitor for 30 minutes to confirm latency stays within SLO.
- File a capacity planning ticket to review autoscaling thresholds.

If scaling out does not resolve:
- Rollback to prior model version (see [Model Rollback runbook](./model-rollback.md))
- Escalate to ML on-call (see below).

## Escalation

**ML On-Call**: Page via PagerDuty tag `ml-oncall`
**SRE On-Call**: Page via PagerDuty tag `sre-oncall`
**Escalate after**: 20 minutes without mitigation path identified

## Post-Incident

After resolution:
- Update this runbook if any step was inaccurate or missing.
- File post-mortem for P1/P2 incidents.
- Note: if you scaled out, verify autoscaling is re-engaged after the incident.
GenAI-Specific Runbook Categories

Standard infrastructure runbooks (high CPU, disk full, network latency) have established patterns. GenAI services require additional runbook categories that address failure modes specific to ML inference pipelines.
Model Output Quality Degradation

Model output quality degradation is one of the hardest failure modes to runbook because the symptoms are detected by evaluation pipelines, not by latency or error rate metrics. The diagnostic steps are correspondingly different:

# Runbook: Model Output Quality Degradation

## Symptom
The automated output quality evaluation pipeline reports that the
`semantic_similarity_score` or `task_accuracy_score` for the production
model has dropped below the alert threshold.

**Alert**: `INFERENCE_QUALITY_DEGRADATION`
**Dashboard**: [Model Quality Metrics](https://grafana.internal/d/model-quality)

## Initial Triage

1. Confirm the degradation is real, not an evaluation pipeline issue:
   ```bash
   # Re-run the quality evaluation manually against the current production model
   python scripts/evaluate_quality.py --environment production --sample-size 100
   ```
   If this also shows degradation: proceed to diagnostic steps.
   If this shows no degradation: the evaluation pipeline is the issue, not the model.
   File a bug against the evaluation pipeline and close this incident.

2. Check for recent model deployments:
   ```bash
   kubectl rollout history deployment/inference-service -n inference
   ```
   If a deployment occurred in the last 24h: go to Step A.
   If no recent deployment: go to Step B.

## Diagnostic Steps

**Step A: Post-deployment quality regression**
Compare output quality between the current version and the prior version:
```bash
python scripts/compare_model_versions.py   --version-a <CURRENT_VERSION>   --version-b <PRIOR_VERSION>   --test-set golden-prompts/quality-eval.jsonl
```
If prior version shows higher quality: rollback (see Model Rollback runbook).

**Step B: Gradual drift (no recent deployment)**
Gradual quality drift without a deployment usually indicates:
- Training distribution shift (user input patterns have changed)
- Model serving infrastructure issue (bit corruption in weights, CUDA error accumulation)
Run the weight integrity check:
```bash
python scripts/verify_model_weights.py --model production
```
If integrity check fails: restart inference pods to reload weights from storage.
If integrity check passes: file ticket for ML team to investigate distribution shift.
Prompt Injection Detection Alert

Prompt injection attempts — user inputs that try to override system prompts or extract confidential instructions — require a specific runbook because the response is not just operational but involves a security decision:

# Runbook: Elevated Prompt Injection Detection Rate

## Symptom
The safety monitoring pipeline reports that the `prompt_injection_detection_rate`
is elevated above the baseline by more than 3x for a sustained 15-minute window.

## Initial Triage

1. Confirm this is an attack, not a false positive spike:
   ```bash
   # Sample the flagged requests (PII-scrubbed)
   python scripts/sample_flagged_requests.py      --type prompt-injection --last-minutes 30 --sample-size 20
   ```
   Review the sample. If requests look like legitimate user inputs that are
   being misclassified: file a false positive ticket for the safety team.
   If requests look like genuine injection attempts: proceed.

2. Identify the source:
   - Single API key: likely automated probe from one actor
   - Multiple API keys, single IP range: likely coordinated probe
   - Distributed sources: likely public vulnerability disclosure targeting this endpoint

## Response Actions

**For single API key:**
```bash
# Temporarily rate-limit the key to 1 request per minute
python scripts/rate_limit_key.py --key <API_KEY> --limit 1/min --reason "prompt-injection-probe"
```

**For coordinated probe (IP range):**
Add the CIDR to the WAF block list. Contact security@company.com before blocking
ranges > /24 (may affect legitimate users).

**Notify**: security@company.com and on-call security engineer via PagerDuty tag `security-oncall`.
Maintenance Triggers and Cadences
Post-Incident Updates — Within 48 Hours

Any incident where the on-call engineer found a runbook inaccurate, incomplete, or missing must result in a runbook update within 48 hours of incident resolution. This is not optional — it is tracked as a P2 action item in the post-mortem.
Common post-incident runbook issues to address:
Gap Type
Example
Fix
Missing diagnostic step
Runbook did not mention checking the vector store health when RAG quality degraded
Add the missing step before the escalation
Stale command
kubectl command referenced old namespace before namespace consolidation
Update command; add comment noting the change date
Wrong escalation contact
ML on-call contact was a personal phone number that changed
Replace with PagerDuty tag
Missing rollback instruction
Runbook described scaling out but did not say how to scale back after incident
Add explicit rollback steps
Ambiguous symptom description
Runbook said 'if latency is high' without specifying the threshold
Add the exact metric, threshold, and alert name
Quarterly Scheduled Review
A quarterly scheduled review verifies that the runbook is accurate in the absence of an incident that surfaced a gap. The review follows a checklist:

#!/bin/bash
# scripts/runbook_review.sh
# Run quarterly by the on-call rotation lead
# For each runbook, verify the following:

RUNBOOK=$1

echo "Reviewing: ${RUNBOOK}"
echo ""
echo "1. Verify all dashboard links are reachable and show correct data:"
# (manual step — open each link from the runbook and verify)

echo "2. Test all diagnostic commands:"
# (manual step — copy commands to staging, run, verify expected output matches)

echo "3. Verify escalation contacts are current:"
# Cross-reference with PagerDuty schedule
python scripts/verify_escalation_contacts.py --runbook "${RUNBOOK}"

echo "4. Verify alert name matches current AlertManager config:"
python scripts/verify_alert_names.py --runbook "${RUNBOOK}"

echo "5. Update last_reviewed_date in runbook metadata:"
python scripts/update_runbook_metadata.py --runbook "${RUNBOOK}" --reviewed-by "$(git config user.email)"

echo "Review complete. Commit and push changes."
Change-Triggered Reviews

When code or infrastructure changes affect what a runbook describes, the runbook must be reviewed before the change reaches production. This is implemented as a CI check that maps changed files to runbooks via tags:

# scripts/runbook_impact_check.py
# Identifies runbooks that may be affected by a given set of code changes
# Integrated into CI pipeline; outputs list of runbooks requiring review

RUNBOOK_TAGS = {
    "app/inference/latency.py":      ["inference-latency-slo-breach"],
    "app/safety/classifier.py":      ["prompt-injection-detection", "safety-filter-behavior"],
    "app/models/loader.py":          ["model-output-quality", "model-rollback"],
    "infra/gpu_config.tf":           ["high-gpu-utilization", "inference-latency-slo-breach"],
    "app/api/rate_limiter.py":       ["rate-limit-429", "prompt-injection-detection"],
    "app/rag/vector_store.py":       ["model-output-quality", "rag-retrieval-failure"],
}

def find_affected_runbooks(changed_files: list[str]) -> set[str]:
    affected = set()
    for f in changed_files:
        for pattern, runbooks in RUNBOOK_TAGS.items():
            if pattern in f:
                affected.update(runbooks)
    return affected

import sys
changed = sys.argv[1:]
affected = find_affected_runbooks(changed)
if affected:
    print("RUNBOOKS REQUIRING REVIEW before this change reaches production:")
    for rb in sorted(affected):
        print(f"  docs/runbooks/{rb}.md")
    print("")
    print("Open a PR to update the listed runbooks, or confirm they are still accurate.")
    sys.exit(1)  # fail CI until runbooks are confirmed reviewed
sys.exit(0)
Fire Drills for Runbook Validation

A fire drill is a planned exercise where the on-call team simulates following a runbook for a hypothetical incident. Fire drills surface gaps that scheduled reviews miss: a command that technically runs but produces confusing output, a diagnostic step that assumes knowledge the on-call engineer does not have, or an escalation path that reaches someone who has changed roles.
Fire Drill Structure
Select a runbook
to drill — prioritize runbooks that have not been invoked in a real incident in the past 6 months (they are most likely to have gaps).
Brief the participants
— explain the scenario without revealing the runbook content. Let the engineer follow the runbook as written rather than from memory.
Execute the runbook steps in a non-production environment
— for commands that require live data, use the staging environment or a synthetic monitoring environment.
Note every friction point
— a step that requires clarification, a command that fails, a link that is broken, or a step that took more than 2 minutes to execute without obvious progress.
Update the runbook immediately after the drill
— friction points become runbook improvements before the drill session ends.
Fire Drill Debrief Template

# Fire Drill Debrief: inference-latency-slo-breach

**Date**: 2025-11-15
**Participants**: Bob Smith (on-call engineer), Carol Davis (observer/note-taker)
**Scenario**: P2 incident — inference latency p95 > 2000ms in eu-west-1

## Runbook Gaps Found

1. **Step A dashboard link was broken**
   - The Grafana URL had changed from /d/genai-inference/latency to /d/inference-v2/latency
   - Fix: Update dashboard link; add a monitoring check on critical runbook links

2. **Step C kubectl command failed in staging**
   - The namespace was `inference-staging` not `inference` in staging
   - Fix: Parameterize namespace with an environment variable; document how to set it

3. **Escalation contact listed personal Slack handle, not PagerDuty tag**
   - The engineer found that paging via Slack DM is unreliable at 2am
   - Fix: Replace with `page via PagerDuty tag ml-oncall`

4. **No instruction on what to do if scaling out does not help**
   - After 15 minutes of no progress, engineer was not sure whether to continue
     investigating or escalate immediately
   - Fix: Add explicit time threshold: "If latency does not improve within 10 minutes
     of scaling out, escalate to ML on-call"

## Time to Resolution (hypothetical)
- With current runbook: estimated 42 minutes to mitigation
- After fixes: estimated 18 minutes (based on drill execution)

## Action Items
- [ ] Fix dashboard link (assigned: Carol, due: 2025-11-16)
- [ ] Fix kubectl namespace (assigned: Carol, due: 2025-11-16)
- [ ] Update escalation contact (assigned: Bob, due: 2025-11-15 — same day)
- [ ] Add time threshold for escalation decision (assigned: Carol, due: 2025-11-16)
Summary

GenAI troubleshooting runbooks decay faster than standard infrastructure runbooks because they describe behaviors that change with model versions, alert thresholds that are tuned over time, and escalation contacts that rotate. The maintenance lifecycle has four components: post-incident updates within 48 hours when a gap is found during a real incident, quarterly scheduled reviews that verify accuracy proactively, change-triggered reviews when code or infrastructure changes affect documented behavior, and biannual fire drills that validate the runbook against a simulated incident.

The structure of the runbook itself determines how maintainable it is. Runbooks that use role-based escalation rather than individual names, reference parameterized alert thresholds rather than hardcoded values, and embed dashboard links rather than navigation instructions can be updated in one place when the underlying system changes — rather than requiring edits scattered across multiple runbooks.

For GenAI-specific failure modes — output quality degradation, prompt injection detection, safety filter behavior changes — standard infrastructure runbooks do not provide adequate guidance. These require dedicated runbooks with diagnostic steps tailored to ML system failure modes, including commands that inspect model weights, evaluate output quality on golden prompts, and sample flagged requests for manual review. Maintaining these runbooks requires collaboration between the on-call engineering rotation and the ML safety and quality teams.
